title: A Deployment-Aware Framework for Carbon- and Water- Efficient LLM Serving
text: <jats:p>Inference now dominates the lifecycle footprint of large language models, yet published estimates often use inconsistent boundaries and optimize carbon while ignoring water. We present a provider-agnostic framework that unifies scope-transparent measurement with time-resolved, SLO-aware orchestration and jointly optimizes carbon and consumptive water. Measurement reports daily medians at a comprehensive serving boundary that includes accelerators, host CPU/DRAM, provisioned idle, and PUE uplift, and provides accelerator-only whiskers for reconciliation. Optimization uses a mixed-integer linear program solved over five-minute windows; it selects region, batch size, and phase-aware hardware for prefill and decode while enforcing p95 TTFT and TPOT as well as capacity constraints. Applied to four representative models, a single SLO-aware policy reduces comprehensive-boundary medians by 57 to 59 percent for energy, 59 to 60 percent for water, and 78 to 80 percent for location-based CO2, with SLOs met in every window. For a day with 500 million queries on GPT-4o, totals fall from 0.344 to 0.145 GWh, 1.196 to 0.490 ML, and 121 to 25 t CO2 (location-based). The framework offers a deployable template for carbon- and water-aware LLM serving with auditable and scope-transparent reporting.</jats:p>

title: Offline Energy-Optimal LLM Serving: Workload-Based Energy Models for LLM Inference on Heterogeneous Systems
text: <jats:p> The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has led to significant advances in natural language processing and text generation. However, the energy consumed through LLM model inference remains a major challenge for sustainable AI deployment. To address this problem, we model the workload-dependent energy consumption and runtime of LLM inference tasks on heterogeneous GPU-CPU systems. By conducting an extensive characterization study of several state-of-the-art LLMs and analyzing their energy and runtime behavior across different magnitudes of input prompts and output text, we develop accurate ( <jats:italic> R <jats:sup>2</jats:sup> </jats:italic> > 0.96) energy and runtime models for each LLM. We employ these models to explore an offline, energy-optimal LLM workload scheduling framework. Through a case study, we demonstrate the advantages of energy and accuracy aware scheduling compared to existing best practices. </jats:p>

title: LOIS: Learning-Optimized Inference System for Cost-Efficient Large Language Model Deployment
text: <jats:p>The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) at scale presents critical economic and operational challenges, with inference costs representing 60-80% of total AI operational expenditure for production systems. Current infrastructure approaches rely on static resource allocation and manual optimization, resulting in significant cost inefficiencies and suboptimal resource utilization. We present LOIS (Learning-Optimized Inference System), a novel selfoptimizing infrastructure framework that dynamically adapts LLM deployment strategies through reinforcement learning and predictive analytics. LOIS incorporates four key innovations: (1) adaptive model routing that intelligently selects optimal models based on query complexity, (2) dynamic quantization that adjusts precision in real-time, (3) predictive resource allocation using workload pattern learning, and (4) multi-tenant optimization that maximizes compute sharing across diverse workloads. Our evaluation combines analytical modeling and discrete-event simulation on production-representative workloads, indicating projected improvements of up to 47% reduction in inference costs, 34% improvement in P95 latency, and 3.2x better GPU utilization compared to baseline Kubernetes orchestration. While results are based on simulation rather than live deployment, the methodology employs empirically-validated performance models and conservative assumptions grounded in published benchmarks. LOIS represents a paradigm shift from reactive infrastructure management to proactive, learning-based optimization, offering a practical architectural foundation for economically sustainable LLM deployment at scale.</jats:p>
